html:
VI

вАЬThese are the things I wanted you to see,вАЭ said Gert Gram, rising. He had been on his knees, looking for something on the lower shelf of his safe.

Jenny pushed the sketchbooks aside and pulled the electric lamp nearer. He wiped the dust from the big portfolio and placed it before her.

вАЬI have not shown these to anybody for a great many years, or looked at them myself, but I have been wanting you to see them for some timeвБ†вАФin fact, from the day I called on you at your studio. When you came here to look at HelgeвАЩs picture I meant to ask you if you cared to see them, and all the time you were working close by here I had it in my mind.

вАЬIt is strange to think, Jenny, that here in this little office I have buried all my dreams of youth. There in the safe they lie like corpses in their tomb, and I myself go about a dead and forgotten artist.вАЭ

Jenny said nothing. Gram sometimes used expressions that were rather too sentimental, she thought, although she knew that the bitter feelings which dictated them were real enough. In a sudden impulse she bent forward and stroked his grey hair.

Gram bowed his head as if to prolong the slight caressвБ†вАФand without looking at her, untied the portfolio with trembling hands.

She realized in surprise that her own hands shook as she took the first sheet from him, and she felt a strange fear and oppression at heart, as of a danger threatening. She was suddenly afraid when she realized that she did not want anybody to know of her visit and that she dared not tell Helge about it. At the mere thought of her lover she became depressed; she had long since consciously stopped analysing her real feelings for him. She did not want to heed the foreboding that crossed her mind at this moment, not to let herself be disturbed by inquiring into Gert GramвАЩs feelings for her.

She turned over the sheets of the portfolio with the dreams of his youth; it was a melancholy business. He had told her about this work often when they were alone, and she understood that he thought he had been born an artist for the sake of this and nothing else. The pictures hanging in his home he called the amateur work of a conscientious and diligent pupil, but theseвБ†вАФthey were his own. They were illustrations to LandstadвАЩs Folksongs.

At first sight these big sheets with frames of Roman foliage and ornate black-letter writing were good enough. The colouring was pure and effective in most of them, in some really fine, but the figures in the vignettes and borders were without style and life, although the miniature drawings were correct in every detail. Some of them were naturalistic, others approaching Italian medieval art to such an extent that Jenny recognized certain Annunciation angels and madonnas in the cloaks of knights and maidens, and the leaves in gold and purple she remembered having seen, in a book of mass in the San Marco library. The words of the songs looked very strange, hand-printed in elegant monastic Latin types. In some of the larger full-page illustrations the composition was baroque, a direct copy of Roman altar pictures. It was an echo of all he had seen and lived among and lovedвБ†вАФan echo of the melody of Gert GramвАЩs youth; not a single note was his own, but this melody of many notes was resounded in a particularly soft, melancholy tone.

вАЬYou donвАЩt quite like them,вАЭ he said. вАЬI can see you donвАЩt.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I like them. There is much that is pretty and delicate about them, but, you knowвАЭвБ†вАФshe searched for the right expressionвБ†вАФвАЬthe effect is a little strange on us, who have seen the same subjects treated differently and so perfectly, that we cannot conceive them treated in another way.вАЭ

He sat opposite her, resting his chin in his hand. By and by he looked up, and she was sad at heart to meet his eyes.

вАЬI seem to remember them as being much better than they are,вАЭ he said quietly, trying to smile. вАЬI have not opened this portfolio for many a year, as I told you.вАЭ

вАЬI have never quite understood that you were so attracted by the later renaissance and the baroque,вАЭ she said, to divert the conversation.

вАЬI am not surprised, Jenny dear, that you donвАЩt understand it.вАЭ He looked into her face with a melancholy smile. вАЬThere was a time when I believed in myself as an artist, but not so completely that I did not have a slight doubt sometimes, not of succeeding to express what I wanted, but as to what I really wanted to express. I saw that romantic art had had its day and was on the declineвБ†вАФthere was decadence and falsehood all alongвБ†вАФand yet in my heart I was devoted to romance, not in painting alone, but in real life. I wanted the Sunday-peasants of romance, although I had lived long enough in the country as a boy to know they did not exist, and when I went abroad it was to the Italy of romance I turned my steps. I know that you and your contemporaries seek beauty in things as they are, tangible and real. To me there was beauty only in the transformation of reality, which had already been done by others. In the eighties there came a new art-creed. I tried to adopt it, but the result was lip-service only, for my heart rebelled against it.вАЭ

вАЬBut reality, Gert, is not a fixed conception. It appears different to everyone who sees it. An English painter once said to me: вАШThere is beauty in everything; only your eyes see it or do not see it.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬI was not made to conceive reality, only the reflection of it in the dreams of others. I lacked entirely the capacity to form a beauty for myself out of the complexity of realities; I knew my own ineffectiveness. When I came to Italy the baroque took my heart and fancy. Can you not understand the agony of my soul on realizing my inefficiency? To have nothing new or personal wherewith to fill up form, only develop the technique in soaring fancies, breakneck foreshortenings, powerful effects of light and shade, and cunningly thought-out compositions. The emptiness of it all is to be hidden under the ecstasyвБ†вАФcontorted faces, twisted limbs, saints, whose only true passion is the dread of their own engulfing doubt, which they try to drown in sickly exaltation. It is the despair of the good, the work of an epigon school wishing to fascinateвБ†вАФmostly themselves.вАЭ

Jenny nodded. вАЬWhat you say, Gert, is at least your own subjective view. I am not so sure that the painters you speak of were not highly pleased with themselves.вАЭ

He laughed and said: вАЬPerhaps they wereвБ†вАФand perhaps this is my hobbyhorse because for once I hadвБ†вАФas you sayвБ†вАФa subjective view.вАЭ

вАЬBut the picture of your wife in red is impressionistic, excellent. The more I look at it, the more I like it.вАЭ

вАЬYes, but that is a solitary instance.вАЭ After a pause вАЬWhen I painted it, she was all the world to me. I was very much in love with herвБ†вАФand I hated her intensely already.вАЭ

вАЬWas it because of her you gave up painting?вАЭ asked Jenny.

вАЬNo. All our misfortunes are of our own making. I know you have not what they call faith, neither have IвБ†вАФbut I believe in a God, if you like, or a spiritual power which punishes justly.

вАЬShe was cashier in a shop in the High Street; I happened to see her there. She was remarkably pretty, as you can see still. One evening when she went home I waited for her and spoke to her. We made friendsвБ†вАФand I seduced her,вАЭ he said in a low and harsh voice.

вАЬAnd you married her because she was going to have a child?вБ†вАФI thought so. For twenty-seven years she has tormented you in return. Do you know what I think of the deity you believe in?вБ†вАФthat it is rather relentless.вАЭ

He smiled wearily. вАЬI am not quite so old-fashioned as you may think. I donвАЩt consider it a sin if two young people, who love and believe in one another, join their lives in a lawful or unlawful way. But in my case I was the abductor. She was innocent when I met herвБ†вАФinnocent in every way. I understood her better than she did herself. I saw that she was passionate, and would be jealous and tyrannical in her love, but I did not care. I was flattered that her passion was for me, that this beautiful girl was all mine, but I never meant to be hers alone, the way I knew she wanted it. I did not exactly mean to leave her, but I thought I should be able to arrange our life in such a way as not to share with her my interests, my work, my real life in fact, as I knew she would want to do. It was stupid of me, knowing that I was weak and she strong and ruthless. I thought that her great passion would give me, who was comparatively cold, a hold on her.

вАЬBeyond her great faculty of loving there was nothing in her. She was vain and uneducated, envious and crude. There could be no mental fellowship between us, but I did not miss it; to possess her beauty and her passionate love was all I cared for.вАЭ

He rose and went over to the side where Jenny was sitting. He took both her hands and pressed them against his eyes.

вАЬWhat else but misery could I expect from a marriage with her? But we reap as we sow, and I had to marry her. I had a dreadful time of it. At first, when she came to my studio, she was proud to be my mistress, arrogant in her denunciation of old prejudices, declaring that the only life worth living was that of free love. The moment things went wrong she changed her tone. Then it was all about her respectable family in Frederikshald, her unstained virtue, and her good reputation. Many men had wanted her, but she had not listened to anybody, and I was a scoundrel and a wretch if I did not marry her at once. I had nothing to marry on; I had neglected my studies and had learnt nothing but painting. Some months went byвБ†вАФat last I had to apply to my father. My people helped me through. We got married, and two months later Helge arrived.

вАЬI had had hopes of a great artistic workвБ†вАФmy folklore illustrationsвБ†вАФbut I had to give up my dreams for the reality of making bread and butter. Once I had to come to an agreement with my creditors. She took her share of the struggle and poverty loyally and without complaintвБ†вАФshe would willingly have starved for me and the children. Feeling as I did towards her, it was hard to accept what she gave in working, suffering, and renouncing for my sake.

вАЬI had to sacrifice everything I loved; she forced me to give it up inch by inch. From the very first she and my father were mortal enemies. He could not bear his daughter-in-law, and that was a blow to her vanity, so she set about to make trouble between him and me. My father was an official of the old schoolвБ†вАФa bit narrow and stiff maybe, but right-minded and loyal, noble and good at heart. You would have liked him, I am sure. We had been so much to one another, but our intimacy was put a stop to.

вАЬAs for my painting, I understood that I had not the talent I once had imagined, and I lacked energy to make continued efforts when I did not believe in myselfвБ†вАФdead tired as I was of the struggle and of my life at her side, which became more and more of a caricature. She reproached me, but secretly she triumphed.

вАЬShe was jealous of the children too, if I was fond of them or they were fond of me. She would not share them with me nor me with them.

вАЬHer jealousy has grown into a kind of madness as the years have gone by. You have seen it for yourself. She can scarcely bear to see me in the same room with you even when Helge is there.вАЭ

Jenny went to him and laid her hands on his shoulders:

вАЬI cannot understand,вАЭ she saidвБ†вАФвАЬI really cannotвБ†вАФthat you have been able to stand such a life.вАЭ

Gert Gram bent forward, resting his head on her shoulder:

вАЬI donвАЩt understand it myself.вАЭ

When he raised his head and their eyes met, she put her hand to his neck and, overwhelmed by a tender compassion, kissed him on the cheek and forehead.

She felt a sudden fear when she looked down at his face resting on her shoulder, with eyes closed, but the next moment he lifted his head and rose, saying:

вАЬThank you, Jenny dear.вАЭ

Gram put the drawings back in their cover and straightened the table.

вАЬI hope you will be very, very happy. You are so bright and courageous, so energetic and gifted. Dear child, you are everything I wanted to be, but never was.вАЭ He spoke in a low, absentminded voice.

вАЬI think,вАЭ he said a moment later, вАЬthat when relations between two people are new, before their life is perfectly accorded, there are many small difficulties to overcome. I wish you could live elsewhere, not in this town. You should be alone, far from your own peopleвБ†вАФat first at least.вАЭ

вАЬHelge has applied for a post in Bergen, as you know,вАЭ said Jenny, and the feeling of despair and anguish again seized her when she thought of him.

вАЬDo you never speak to your mother about it? Why donвАЩt you? Are you not fond of your mother?вАЭ

вАЬOf course I am fond of her.вАЭ

вАЬI should think it would be a good thing to talk to her about itвБ†вАФget her advice.вАЭ

вАЬIt is no good asking anybodyвАЩs adviceвБ†вАФI donвАЩt like to speak to anyone about these things,вАЭ she said, wishing to dismiss the subject.

вАЬNo, you are perhaps.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He had been standing halfway turned to the window. Suddenly his face changed, and he whispered in a state of excitement:

вАЬJenny, she is down there in the street!вАЭ

вАЬWho?вАЭ

вАЬSheвБ†вАФRebecca!вАЭ

Jenny rose. She felt she could have screamed with exasperation and disgust. She trembled; every fibre of her body was quivering with revolt. She would not be involved in all thisвБ†вАФthese wicked, odious suspicions, quarrels, spiteful words, and scenesвБ†вАФno, she would not.

вАЬJenny, my child, you are shiveringвБ†вАФdonвАЩt be afraid. I wonвАЩt let her hurt you.вАЭ

вАЬAfraid? Far from it.вАЭ She steeled herself at once. вАЬI have been here to fetch you; we have looked at your drawings, and we are now going to your house to supper.вАЭ

вАЬShe may not have noticed anything.вАЭ

вАЬHeavens! we have nothing to hide. If she had not seen that I am here she will soon get to know it. I am going with you; we must do it for your sake as well as for mineвБ†вАФdo you hear?вАЭ

Gram looked at her: вАЬYes, let us go, then.вАЭ

When they got down in the street Mrs.¬†Gram was gone.

вАЬLet us take the tram, Gert; it is late,вАЭ she said, adding in a sudden temper: вАЬOh, we must stop all thisвБ†вАФif only for HelgeвАЩs sake.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Gram opened the door. Gert Gram ventured an explanation; Jenny looked frankly into the angry eyes of his wife: вАЬI am sorry Helge is out for the evening. Do you think he will be home early?вАЭ

вАЬI am surprised you did not remember it,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Gram said to her husband. вАЬIt is no pleasure to Miss Winge to sit here with us two old people.вАЭ

вАЬOh, that is all right,вАЭ said Jenny.

вАЬI donвАЩt remember hearing that Helge was going out this evening,вАЭ said Gram.

вАЬFancy your coming without any needlework,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Gram, when they were sitting in the drawing-room after supper. вАЬYou are always so industrious.вАЭ

вАЬI left the studio so late, I had no time to go home in between. Perhaps you could find me something?вАЭ

Jenny conversed with Mrs.¬†Gram about the price of embroidery patterns at home and in Paris, and about books she had lent her. Gram was reading. Now and again she felt his eyes on her. Helge returned about eleven.

вАЬWhat is the matter?вАЭ he asked, when they walked down the stairs. вАЬHas there been a scene again?вАЭ

вАЬNo, not at all,вАЭ she replied, in a short, irritated voice. вАЬI suppose your mother did not like my coming home with your father.вАЭ

вАЬIt seems to me, too, that you need not have done it,вАЭ said Helge humbly.

вАЬI am going home by tram.вАЭ Overwrought, and unable to control herself, she pulled her arm out of his. вАЬI cannot stand any more tonight, and I will not have these scenes with you every time I have been to your home. Good night.вАЭ

вАЬJenny! Wait! Jenny!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He hurried after her, but she was already at the stop when the tram came, and got in, leaving him without a word.
